content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Donna Mooney 
Chief Assistant City Attorney· 
City Attorney's Office 
555 Santa Clara Street 
Vallejo, CA 94590 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-076 
Dear Ms. Mooney: 
May 15, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Vallejo City Councilmember 
Robert McConnell regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 
"Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts µnderlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May Councilmember McConnell participate in the Accessory Dwelling Unit ('"ADU") 
ordinance decision where he owns five properties that have the potential for an ADU? 
CONCLUSION 
No, he may not participate. Under the Act, he has a reasonably foreseeable and material 
financial interest in the ADU ordinance decision. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR 
File No. A-18-076 
Page No. 2 
The Vallejo City Council will be considering whether to amend its zoning code with an 
ordinance setting forth certain requirements for ADUs. The amendment proposed by staff for City 
Council consideration would not add or delete any zoning districts where ADUs might be built. 
The amendments would clarify that the lot on which an ADU is desired must be zoned for 
residential use, and that only one primary unit may exist on the lot. The amendment clarifies the 
height limit for a detached ADU, amends the floor area limit, establishes a minimum side and rear 
yard setback for units built above a garage, establishes access requirements, retains the requirement 
that the property owner live in one of the units and that the restriction be recorded, and amends 
parking requirements. 
Councilmember McConnell owns five properties in the City, four residential and one 
commercial that may be eligible for the construction of an ADU under the proposed zoning 
ordinance. Councilmember McConnell's four residential properties that 1) are located in zoning 
districts that allow ADUs as the ordinance is presently written and under the proposed amendments 
and 2) have sufficient space to build an ADU under the present ordinance and proposed 
amendments. The proposed amendment does not change the requirement that a property owner 
must live on the lot in either the main residence or in the ADU, and if the City Council determines 
to retain that requirement, Councilmember McConnell would not be able to construct an ADU on 
all four properties. 
Also, although there is sufficient physical space to construct an ADU on the commercial 
property, it is zoned as Linear Commercial which does not allow residential use, so therefore under 
the present ordinance and proposed amendments he would not be permitted to construct an ADU 
there. All of Councilmember McConnell's remaining residential properties (other than the four 
referenced above), while properly zoned for an ADU, physically have insufficient space to add an 
ADU. In total, Councilmember McConnell owns 13 properties. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the 
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to infl~ence a governmental decision in 
which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a 
"financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally, on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section. As applicable to the 
facts provided, Section 87103 identifies the following applicable interests: 
• 
Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or 
more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is a director; officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• 
Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b).) 


File No. A-18-076 
Page No. 3 
• 
Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received 
by, the official within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
For the decision at issue, Councilmember McConnell has an interest in his rental business as 
a business entity and source of income, the tenants of his rentals as sources of income, and in the 
five parcels as real property. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A decision's financial effect on an official's interest is presumed reasonably foreseeable if 
the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) An interest is 
the "subject" of a proceeding, and deemed materially affected, when the decision determines the 
parcel's zoning or rezoning (other than a zoning decision applicable to all properties designated in 
that category). (Regulation 18702.2(a)(2).) 
Councilmember McConnell's real property is the subject of this decision regarding the 
staffs proposed zoning amendments. His five parcels are currently in the areas zoned for ADUs 
and the decision on the amendments will determine ifhe may have ADUs on these parcels. 
Therefore, the effect of dec~sion relating to the proposed zoning ordinance on the Councilmember's 
interests in each of those properties is presumed reasonably foreseeable, and material, under 
Regulation 18702.2(a)(2).2 He may not participate in this decision due to his conflict of interest 
under the Act. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
LKH:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Acting General Counsel 
L 4!~ 
By: 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division 
2 We do not further analyze the effect of the proposed zoning ordinance on the Councilmember's business 
interests or source of income, including tenants as a source of income, because we have detcnnined that tJ1e 
Councilmember is disqualified from participating based on his real property interests.
